coredata.dc:title: Sugar-coated cannonballs for effective antibacterial treatments but with negligible cytotoxicity based on silver (copper) -containing carbon dots and their mechanism analysis

Huang, Chao
Yue, Wenhui
Wang, Xiaoning
Sun, Yiping
Bi, Weilin
Wang, Lu
Xu, Yuanhong
coredata.dc:description: With the gradual development of carbon dots (CDs) in the antimicrobic application, efforts have been still devoted to overcome the dilemma that more effective antibacterial treatment would lead to inevitable higher cytotoxicity. In addition, their bactericidal mechanisms are still in urgent need to be clarified. Therefore, silver (copper)-containing carbon dots have been prepared via one microwave-irradiation strategy using glucose as precursors and metal salts as passivators. It was discovered that the as-prepared metal-containing CDs have the comparable or better bactericidal effect while lower cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells compared with most of corresponding commercial metal-containing antibacterial agents and their free metal ions, because of the good permeability towards bacterial cells originating from the excellent metal dispersion and glucose disguiser in/on the CDs. The minimum bactericidal concentration of silver(copper)-containing carbon dots against 1.5 (0.5) × 107 CFU/mL Escherichia coli was less than 25 (75) μg/mL. Different bactericidal mechanisms between the two metal-containing CDs and the free copper/silver ions were revealed in details. It was found that the metal ions could directly inhibit the function of ribosomes in bacterial cells, while the metal-containing CDs showed negligible influence towards ribosome directly, instead, they inhibited bacterial metabolism through more complex pathways such as metabolic disorders, transporter inactivation or inhibition of the nucleic acid synthesis. Since the mammalians cells can switch to other pathways more flexibly than bacterial cells under the disturbance of the same metabolism pathway inhibition, the metal-containing CDs provides excellent biocompatibility.

(A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) sterilizing ratio of AgCD and (b) CuCD, (c) bactericidal effect of AgCD comparing with A1 and A2, (d) bactericidal effect of CuCD comparing with copper sulfate, (e) MTT experiment of AgCD, A1, A2, silver nitrate, CuCD and copper sulfate, (f) bactericidal effect of metal-CDs cultured with thiourea. The concentration located at the bottom represented the silver content of A1/A2 that equal to the silver content of AgCD. And the concentrations at the top were AgCD total concentrations. All concentrations were the final concentrations effecting on bacteria. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM and corresponding integrated and sliced bacterial TEM images of bacterial morphology treated by control group, AgCD group and CuCD group, (b) fluorescent images of bacteria through live/dead kit for AgCD group and CuCD group. MBC concentrations of the two metal-CDs were employed for the experiment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Protein leakage of AgCD and (b) CuCD treated bacteria by standard Bradford assay, (c) membrane depolarization experiments for AgCD treated bacteria and (d) CuCD treated bacteria. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Element dispersed images of (a) control group, (b) AgCD group and (c) CuCD group for corresponding element on bacteria. Scanning represents no element but the simple scanning for bacteria. Ag Lα1 represents the existent of silver element if the image was colored, which are the same as Cu Kα1 (copper) and C Kα1(carbon). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) DNA gel electrophoresis of AgCD treated bacteria and (b) CuCD treated bacteria, (c) LDH viability of AgCD treated bacteria and CuCD treated bacteria, (d) bacterial metabolic activity of AgCD treated bacteria and CuCD treated bacteria, (e) total ROS generation of AgCD treated bacteria and CuCD treated bacteria, (f) MDA release of AgCD treated bacteria and CuCD treated bacteria.

(a) Up/down regulated gene numbers and (b) volcano plot of Ag group, (c)GO enrichment and (d) KEGG pathway enrichment for AgCD treated bacteria, (e) up/down regulated gene numbers and (f) volcano plot of Cu group, (g) GO enrichment and (h) KEGG pathway enrichment for CuCD treated bacteria.

(a) Metabolism pathway of fatty acid degradation (ko00017) for AgCD group, (b) Metabolism pathway of one carbon pool (ko00670) by folate for CuCD group. One arrow represents one DEG, and red color means up-regulation while green color means down-regulation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Analysis of DEGs of bacteria treated by AgCD: (a) metabolisms of amino acid and fatty acid, (b) membrane transport and cellular mobility (c) oxidative stress protection, (d) unique PTS comparing with silver nitrate; and analysis of DEGs for CuCD treated bacteria: (e) Nucleotide and nucleic acid metabolism disorder, (f) membrane transport, (h) cellular motility and oxidative stress and (g) inhibition of transcriptional regulation.
Bactericidal mechanisms for metal-CDs toward E. coli using both biochemical index (with gray full line and row) and transcriptome analysis (with black full line and row).

head.ce:title: Sugar-coated cannonballs for effective antibacterial treatments but with negligible cytotoxicity based on silver (copper) -containing carbon dots and their mechanism analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the gradual development of carbon dots (CDs) in the antimicrobic application, efforts have been still devoted to overcome the dilemma that more effective antibacterial treatment would lead to inevitable higher cytotoxicity. In addition, their bactericidal mechanisms are still in urgent need to be clarified. Therefore, silver (copper)-containing carbon dots have been prepared via one microwave-irradiation strategy using glucose as precursors and metal salts as passivators. It was discovered that the as-prepared metal-containing CDs have the comparable or better bactericidal effect while lower cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells compared with most of corresponding commercial metal-containing antibacterial agents and their free metal ions, because of the good permeability towards bacterial cells originating from the excellent metal dispersion and glucose disguiser in/on the CDs. The minimum bactericidal concentration of silver(copper)-containing carbon dots against 1.5 (0.5) × 107 CFU/mL Escherichia coli was less than 25 (75) μg/mL. Different bactericidal mechanisms between the two metal-containing CDs and the free copper/silver ions were revealed in details. It was found that the metal ions could directly inhibit the function of ribosomes in bacterial cells, while the metal-containing CDs showed negligible influence towards ribosome directly, instead, they inhibited bacterial metabolism through more complex pathways such as metabolic disorders, transporter inactivation or inhibition of the nucleic acid synthesis. Since the mammalians cells can switch to other pathways more flexibly than bacterial cells under the disturbance of the same metabolism pathway inhibition, the metal-containing CDs provides excellent biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Since bacterial infections threaten human health, many bactericides have been developed for daily sterilization or superficial disinfection [1,2]. Among all bactericides, carbon dots have been reported as a new-type of environmentally-green material as a bactericide itself. Different from traditional bactericide like quaternary ammonium salts or metal preparations, carbon dots own excellent biocompatibility, easy modification of oxygen groups, large specific surface area and low cytotoxicity [3–5]. Their bactericidal mechanism of photo thermal therapy (PTT) is mainly due to their reactive oxygen species (ROS) inducing optical activity [6,7], and their advantages as carrier of other bactericides have also been reported. However, its bactericidal effect is always light-dependent and the bactericidal strength is usually lower than that of traditional bactericide. Usually, the initial bacterial concentration at the carbon dots is two to three orders of magnitude lower than that of traditional bactericide [8,9]. As a result, the conception of using carbon dots to modify other bactericides has been proposed, and carbon dots usually act as carriers or modifiers to enhance the biocompatibility of bactericides and reduce cytotoxicity [4,10–14]. Among all bactericides, metal nanoparticles or metal-containing complex have been always modified or prepared using carbon-based materials to avoid directly stimulating biological tissue [15].

Metal has a long history as a common bactericidal material [10,11,16]. Silver or copper contained bactericides are the most commonly used for external skin or mucosa sterilization in clinic treatment, and can also be used as fruiter sterilization in agriculture [17]. The reason why these metals are suitable for the preparation of antibacterial agents is that some metal have excellent catalytic activity, smaller size, large surface area and electric or optical properties [10,11]. But metal preparations suffer from high cytotoxicity and bactericidal instability in long-time storage. The participation of carbon dots in metal-containing bactericide not only enhances the dispersibility of metals, but also reduces the content of metals and decreases the risk of drug resistance [10,11,13,18,19]. Metal-carbon materials combine the inherent bactericidal effect with excellent biocompatibility, which is a feasible scheme to reduce the toxicity of metals to cells and improve the bactericidal effect of carbon dots.

Usually, metals perform bactericidal effect including preventing cell wall synthesis, enzyme action, damaging protein and DNA, and inducing ROS [10,15]. To be specific, metals, whether in an ionic or combined status, have a positive charge, which causes them to accumulate on negatively charged cell walls or membranes [12,20]. Metals are also regarded as a Lewis acid (or electron accepter) which has a tendency to react with Lewis base (or electron donors) including protein, enzyme or nucleic acid [12,15,21]. Metals can occupy the active center of enzymes, inducing the production of superfluous ROS to destroy cells because of oxidative stress [10,12,13]. In the existing studies, there is not much distinction between the differences in the bactericidal mechanisms of metal ions and metal contained complexes, such as modified metal nanoparticles and metal-carbon materials [13,14,22]. The mechanisms of both metal ions and the same metal-containing nanomaterials were known as very similar though the metals may have different existing forms in different metal-containing nanomaterials. Moreover, bacterial death caused by different antibacterial agents may show the identical phenomenon. Though carbon materials as modifiers can mitigate direct cytotoxicity and enhance the dispersion of metals, the reason for this phenomenon at the molecular level is still blurred. It is generally believed that residual groups like oxygen groups can neutralize the cytotoxicity of metals by coating them. The main bactericidal effect comes from the release of metal ions, which may act as sustained release in a low concentration of metals [11,14,20,22–24].

Transcriptome analysis was used for deeper mechanisms of metal and carbon materials binding in degree of metabolism. It has been reported that in terms of bactericidal mechanisms, inhibition of respiration, two-component system differentially expressional genes downregulation, metabolism decrease of sugar and amino acid and upregulation of oxidative stress differentially expressional genes were found in metal-dominated bactericidal agents [17,25–27]. Meanwhile, the changes of osmotic pressure, interference with nucleic acid synthesis, inhibition of energy metabolism, biosynthesis of peptidoglycan, upregulation of oxidative stress genes, and decrease of ribosome biosynthesis and tricarboxylic acid cycle were observed in carbon dominated bactericidal agents [28–32]. However, differences between only metal and metal engineering materials (such as metal-containing carbon dots) are not clearly explained, although the possible similarities have been studied in pathways relating to oxidative stress changes, damage of membrane and damage of proteins and nucleic acid [17,25,28–33].

To figure out the different antibacterial mechanisms of metals and metal-containing carbon materials, two typical metals with inherent antibacterial activity were selected. And carbon dot is chosen as a carbonic skeleton and a reductant for metal salt during synthesizing process, because of its simple structure, easy to obtain, good modification performance. And it is a suitable model to study the mechanism of carbon-based models. The antibacterial effect and cytotoxicity of metal ions, mental nanoparticles, metal-containing carbon dots and metal-containing domestic antibacterial agents were compared. In order to better understanding the differences of antibacterial action, bactericidal effect and cytotoxicity of mammal cells, the possible bactericidal mechanisms of different forms of metals were analyzed by combining biochemical factors with the transcriptome analysis. The antibacterial action, biochemical factors and transcriptome analysis of metal-containing carbon dots (metal-CDs) were comprehensively analyzed, which provided detailed support information for the degree of antibacterial effect and molecule degree. Scheme 1 outlines the bactericidal mechanisms of silver(copper)-containing carbon dots.

Glucose, citric acid, fructose, galactose, silver nitrate, copper chloride, copper sulfate, sulfuric acid, quinine sulfate dihydrate, potassium chloride, thiocarbamide, ethyl alcohol, 2.5% glutaraldehyde, silver nanoparticles powder (10–30 nm, 60–100 nm), copper nanoparticles powder(10–30 nm, 60–100 nm) 3- (4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Macklin Co. Ltd. 3,3‘-dipropylthiadicarbocyanine iodide (diSC35) dye was purchase from Aladdin Co.Ltd. Yeast extract and tryptone were purchased from OXOID Ltd. Bradford protein assay kit, total glutathione assay kit, lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) cytotoxicity kit, reactive oxygen species assay kit, lipid peroxidation malondialdehyde (MDA) assay kit, ROS assay kit, SDS lysis buffer were purchased from Beyotime Ltd. Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and staphylococcus aureus(S. aureus, ATCC25923) were purchased from Qingdao Haibo Biology Ltd. Agarose, 6x DNA loading buffer, 4S red plus nucleic acid stain, DNA molecular weight standard marker (100–5000 bp), 50x TAE buffer and bacterial genome DNA extraction kit were purchased from Sangon biotech Co. Ltd. Alamar blue assay kit was purchased from Yeasen biotech Co. Ltd. Invitrogen live/dead baclight™ bacterial viability kit was purchased from Thermo fisher scientific. L929 cell lines were obtained from the Institute of Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 120 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6390LV, Japan) was used to observed microstructural changes of bacteria. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) with energy disperse spectroscopy (Zeiss Sigma 500, Germany) was used to analyzed elements in bacteria. X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterizations were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with a copper Ka (k = 0.154056 nm) radiation source. X-ray photo-electronspectroscopy (XPS) measurements were obtained using a PHI5000 Versaprobe-II spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Ka (1486.6 eV) source. The photoluminescence spectra were obtained using an FS5 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). The fluorescence images for bacteria were recorded with an upright fluorescence microscope (Ni–U eclipse, Nikon, Japan). Fourier-transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd.) were employed for IR spectra. Atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker Co. Ltd.) was used to obtain AFM images. The particle size distribution and zeta potential were studied by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., U.K.). Inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Perkinelmer 2000ICP, USA) were employed for analysis of mental contents. A multifunctional microwell plate enzyme marking instrument (Spark, TECAN, Switzerland) was employed for colorimetric and fluorescent value reading from kit assays. An electrophoresis meter (Bio-Aad PowerPac Basic, USA) was used for DNA gel electrophoresis. Two kinds of silver containing germicide (named A1 and A2) were purchased from domestic stores which used for skin clean. A household microwave oven (Galanz, 900W with 2450 MHz, type: G90F25CN3LN-C2(T1), China) was employed for the synthesis of metal-CDs.

1g glucose and 0.2 g silver nitrate (or copper chloride) were diluted in 15 mL deionized water in 250 mL beaker. The mixture was treated with ultrasonic cleaning equipment for 1 min at 600 W. Then the mixture was placed in a household microwave (around 900 W) for 2 min 50 s–3 min heating. Till the water was dried and the mixture were carbonized that the solid under beaker was expanded slightly. The solid was diluted by water and the undissolved substance was filtered by 0.22 μm millipore filters. The liquid was dialyzed (500 Da) for 24 h and freeze-dried after dialysis. Obtained brown powders were named silver-containing carbon dots (AgCD) or copper-containing carbon dots (CuCD). Except glucose, other small molecule carbon source such as fructose and galactose were used the same synthesis method.

The bacterial freeze-dried powder was diluted. Then, streak plate was prepared for the growth of the single bacterial colonies. After around 18–24 h culture at 37 °C, 3 or 4 single colonies were picked up and turned into liquid culture medium at 37 °C with 150 rpm (rotate speed per minute). After culturing overnight, the cloudy liquid (OD600 = 1 or 2–3 × 109 CFU/mL) was obtained. The liquid medium contained 0.5 g tryptone, 0.5 g sodium chloride and 0.25 g yeast extract per 50 mL sterile water. And the solid medium needed 0.75 g agar additionally per 50 mL sterile water comparing with liquid ones. The concentration of bacteria were titrated by McLeod nephelometer for 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL. Then bacterial liquid was diluted at 1.5 × 107 CFU/mL and 0.5 × 107 CFU/mL for bactericidal experiments. After every five bacterial passage, bacteria liquid needs relining using the primary bacterial batch in order to maintaining genetic material consistently.

AgCD and silver containing germicide use 1.5 × 107 CFU/mL bacterial liquid as experimental concentration while CuCD and copper sulfate use 0.5 × 107 CFU/mL. Concentration gradient of bactericides was designed as 25 μg/mL per grade. For example, 125, 100, 75, 50, 25 and 0 μg/mL of gradients were used for bacterial sterilization. Same volume of bacterial liquid and CDs or other bactericides were mixed and stored at 37 °C for 10 min (for AgCD and corresponding bactericides) or 30 min (for CuCD and copper sulfate). After that, 100 μL mixture was added on solid medium and coated uniformly. The solid medium plates were kept at 37 °C for 24 h to see bactericidal results.

Minimal bactericidal concentration (MBC), which means the bactericide concentration of killing 99% bacterial clump counts, was obtained using the above experimental condition [34]. Other bactericides (such as silver containing germicide or copper sulfate) comparing with metal-containing CDs for bactericidal effect were also obtained.

Metal content (according to the ICP-OES results for metal-containing CDs) were as a standard for calculating and converting the concentration of other bactericides that would make sure the same metal content between CDs and corresponding bactericides.

The bactericidal effect for metal-sheltering CDs was also tested. Thiourea as a copper/silver masking agent was used to silence the possible bactericidal effect from metal [25]. Metal-CDs: thiouea (w:w) = 2 : 1 was accepted, and the two materials were dispersed in 10 mL deionized water and stirred for 1 h. Dialysis and freeze-drying were performed after stirring.
In addition, CDs using different carbon source were also tested for bactericidal effects according to the above experimental condition.

Antibacterial behavior was practiced by a 96-well plate. 1.5 × 108 CFU/ml (for AgCD) or 0.5 × 108 CFU/ml (for CuCD) E. coli 20 μL were added into 96-well plates. Then, liquid culture medium 160 μL and metal-containing CDs 20 μL were added. The final bacterial concentration was 1.5 × 107 CFU/ml or 0.5 × 107 CFU/ml for AgCD or CuCD. And final concentrations of metal-CDs were kept at 500, 250, 125, 62.5, 31.25 and 15.625 μg/mL. Put 96-well plates into incubator at 37 °C and record O.D. value for every 6 h at 620 nm [35]. As for S. aureus, the final concentrations of bacteria were half of E. coli employed in both sterilizing experiment and antibacterial experiment.

DiSC35 dye were diluted in ethyl alcohol for 3 mM firstly, and 0.4 μM DiSC35 dye were prepared using 100 mM potassium chloride [36]. The amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 40 and 120 folds for AgCD and CuCD respectively. The untreated bacterial cells were washed and added 0.4 μM DiSC35 dye into a 96-hole plate for 1 h culturing at 37 °C. Extra dye was washed after loading, and metal-CDs were added at their MBC concentrations. The fluorescent excitation value was 622 nm and the emission value was 670 nm. The fluorescent intensity was recorded every 10 min.

SYTO9 (3.43 mM, DMSO diluted) and PI (20 mM, DMSO diluted) were picked out for 1 and 2 μL respectively into 250 μL water for mixing as the stock dye. Every 1 μL stock dye was couple with every 10 μL bacterial liquid. The amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 40 and 120 folds for AgCD and CuCD respectively. Bacterial cells were firstly treated with metal-CDs for 10 or 30 min, then were washed to retain cell pellets. The pellets were dispersed in 50∼100 μL sterile water, then stock dye was added with the above proportion for 15 min. After coculturing with dyes, suck up 3 μL mixed liquid and drop onto microscope slide for observation at the green (which contained excitation wavelength = 485 ± 10 nm, emission wavelength = 530 ± 12.5 nm) and red channel (which contained excitation wavelength = 485 ± 10 nm, emission wavelength = 620 ± 20 nm) after cover glass being coated.

Standard Bradford assay was used for testing the protein leakage after bacterial cells were treated by metal-CDs [37]. Coommassie Brilliant Blue (G-250) can combine with alkaline or aromatic amine acid of protein under acidic circumstance and color changes resulting from the binding could be detected at 595 nm absorbance. The amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 20 and 60 folds for AgCD and CuCD respectively. In brief, the standard curve was drawn at first by a series of different concentration standard protein combining with G-250. Bacterial cells were treated by metal-CDs for 10 or 30 min and were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. Supernatants were collected for the assay. 100 μL supernatant and 150 μL G-250 were added into every hole of a 96-well plate. The absorbance values were detected after 5 min. And the recorded data were calculated by the formula of standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA agarose gel electrophoresis for fragmentation
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial DNA were extracted by a bacterial DNA extraction kit and extract were stored at −20 °C. The amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 100 and 300 folds for AgCD and CuCD respectively. In this experiment, bacterial cells were treated by metal-CDs for 10 and 30 min. And the concentration of metal-CDs was MBC. 0.25 g agarose and 2.5 μL 4S red plus nucleic acid stain were added into 25 mL of 1x TAE buffer for heating until all substances were dissolved [31]. And the gel was made after the liquid dried out to solid. 1 μL 6x DNA loading buffer mixed with 5 μL extract or standard marker. And mixtures were added into the hole of gel one by another according to different groups (marker, control and test). Then, run 30–40 min under 120 voltage. The gel was pictured by a camera under ultraviolet light.

The activity of LDH was tested by LDH cytotoxicity assay kit. Usually, cell damage results the release of LDH to the medium. LDH can catalyze NAD + into NADH, and NADH can further react with INT and diaphorase to produce red formazan. The production of formazan can be detected at 490 nm for adsorption. In this case, the amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 10 and 30 folds for AgCD and CuCD respectively. The standard curve was drawn at first. And the O.D. value of the control and test groups were calculated and converted into actual content. Bacterial cells were treated by metal-CDs and the supernatants were collected for further kit procedure. The concentration gradient of AgCD was 125, 100, 75, 50, 25, and 0 μg/mL. And the concentration gradient of CuCD was 200, 150, 100, 50, and 0 μg/mL. A 96-well plate was used for the detection of O.D. value.

Total ROS production of bacteria was detected by ROS assay kit. Nonfluorescent DCFH-DA as a probe can penetrate cell membrane and can be turned into fluorescent DCFH by cellular esterase to be detected at 525 nm emission wavelength (the excitation wavelength was set at 488 nm) [38]. The amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 10 folds for both AgCD and CuCD. Firstly, DCFG-DA probe was diluted 1000 folds and was cultured with bacterial liquid for 30 min at 37 °C. Then extra probe was centrifuged and washed by water. And the bactericidal process was proceeded with different concentration of metal-CDs. Finally, the treated bacterial liquid was added into a 96-well plate and tested by a multifunctional enzyme marking instrument.

The production of MDA usually due to the oxidative stress, which was determined by lipid peroxidation MDA assay kit. MDA can react with thiobarbituric acid to produce red substance that can be quantified with a multifunctional enzyme marking instrument at 535 nm for absorbance. The amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 40 and 120 folds for AgCD and CuCD separately. After treated with metal-CDs, the cell pellet was obtained and was treated by SDS lysis buffer at 4 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 4 °C for 10000 rpm for 10 min. And the supernatant was collected for testing. The standard curve was drawn, and the actual content of MDA was calculated according to the linear equation.

The microstructure of bacterial cells before and after treatment by metal-CDs were studied by SEM and TEM images. The amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 10 and 30 folds for AgCD and CuCD separately. Bacterial cells were treated with metal-CDs and the pellets were collected. 2.5% glutaraldehyde 50 μL was added and infiltrate pellets over night at 4 °C without light. Samples for TEM image could directly drop onto the copper screen for observation. However, samples for SEM image needs further process. Briefly, glutaraldehyde was centrifuged and removed. The pellets were dispersed into 30% ethyl alcohol for 15 min. After that, 30% ethyl alcohol was removed and 50% ethyl alcohol was added and stored for 15 min. And the rest can be done in the same manner with 70%,90% and 100% ethyl alcohol one by another every 15 min. Finally, bacterial pellets were dispersed in 100% ethyl alcohol. Drop the mixture onto the silicon wafer. After ethyl alcohol volatilizing, gold powder was sprayed onto the surface of silicon sample for observation.

MTT assay was performed cytotoxicity of CDs and other bactericides. L929 cells were employed as model cells. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was used as a cultural condition at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in an incubator. Briefly, L929 cells were cultured and added 100 μL (contain around 10000 cells) into a 96-well plate for each hole. The plate was shaken following “8” shape to obtain a uniform cell distribution in every hole. After culturing 12 h, the medium was removed, and the bactericide-contained medium was added. The design of concentration gradient was the same as the bactericidal experiment. After being incubated for another 24 h, 20 μL MTT solution were added for each hole, and continued incubated for 4–6 h at 37 °C. After that, the medium was removed and 100 μL DMSO was added for a 10 min storing. The untreated ones were regarded as the control group. Finally, the absorbance of Formazan was read at 492 nm. Concentrations of metal-CDs were designed as 1, 10, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL. And the concentrations of corresponding metal-related bactericides were converted to the same metal content according to ICP-OES results. The relative cell viabilities (%) after treatment with carbon quantum dots at different concentrations were calculated [39]:
Relative
viability
( % )
=
OD sample
OD control
× 100 %

Alamar blue assay was employed for bacterial metabolic activity assessment after treated with metal-CDs. Oxidized Alamar blue has no fluorescence [40]. Some coenzyme or dehydrogenase such as NADPH/NADP, FADH/FAD and NADH/NAD were secreted much when cells have a high metabolic activity. Alamar blue could react with these coenzyme or dehydrogenase and was turned itself into reductive fluorescent structure (λ(ex) = 530, λ(em) = 590). In this case, the amount of bacteria liquid and metal-containing CDs were equally amplified 5 folds for AgCD and CuCD respectively. Both positive (100% reductive Alamar blue) and negative (just medium) control groups were needed.

Bacterial cells were treated with metal-CDs at the concentration of 90% sterilizing ratio so as to silver nitrate and copper sulfate. And cell pellets were collected and stored at −80 °C for RNA extraction. Total RNA was extracted by RNA extraction kit (TIANGEN Co. Ltd.). And The mRNA with polyA tail was enriched by magnetic beads of OligodT, while rRNA was removed through DNA hybridization probe and DNaseI enzyme. cDNA libraries were sequenced on the Illumina sequencing platform. Raw data were filtered using SOAPnuke software (v1.5.2. https://github.com/BGI-flexlab/SOAPnuke) which the joint contamination, unknow N basic groups (which more than 10%) and low-quality reads (reads with a mass value of less than 15 accounted for more than 50% of the total base number). After obtaining clean reads, the data were compared with reference genes using HISAT(Hierarchical Indexing for Spliced Alignment of Transcripts, v2.1.0, http://www.ccb.jhu.edu/software/hisat). And Bowtie2 (v2.2.5, http://bowtie-bio.sourceforge.net/Bowtie2/index.shtml) was used to align clean reads to reference gene sequences, followed by RSEM (v1.3.1) to calculate gene and transcript expression levels. The FPKM value was used to assess the expression levels of unigenes. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were determined using DEseq2 (v1.4.5), which set the q-value (adjusted P-value) ≤ 0.05. The log2|fold change| ≥1.5 and q-value <0.05 were set as the threshold for DEGs analysis. Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genome (KEGG) enrichment analysis were used for further exploration of gene functions relating to phenotypic change. We classified differential genes into biological pathways and used the phyper function in R software for enrichment analysis. Escherichia coli str. K-12 substr. MG1655 was as reference species for transcriptome analysis.

The QY of metal-CDs was calculated by a relative quantum yield calculating method [41]. In this method, quinine sulfate as a reference standard with 0.54 value when the excitation light is around 345 nm. To be specific, 0.0157 g quinine sulfate was diluted into 20 mL 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid. And metal-CDs solution was prepared at least for five different concentrations with a concentration gradient of c, 0.8 c, 0.6 c, 0.4 c and 0.2 c (set ‘c’ as the highest concentration of the 5 tested solution). Both the quinine and the carbon dots solution were no more than 0.1 or 0.05 under the ultraviolet–visible light under corresponding the value of excitation wavelength. The equation was followed as:
φ x

)

2
Where the x and ST refer to text solution and reference solution separately. φ represent QY value. Grad represents the slope of the integrated fluorescent intensity dividing the corresponding absorbance testing by ultraviolet–visible light. And η refers to solvent refractive index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of metal content by ICP-OES
ce:section.ce:para: Standard solutions of silver ion and copper ion were prepared at concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 μg/mL for establishing standard linear curves. Metal-CDs powder was measured (m/mg) and dispersed in the complex of water and acid including 10 mL water, 5 mL 1% nitric acid, 1 mL 30% hydrogen peroxide and 0.5 mL hydrofluoric acid in a 50 mL beaker. The complex was heated at 300 °C and kept for 5 min, and then condensed the solution volume at around 2 mL. After cooling down to room temperature, water was added for a certain volume (V/mL) finally before testing. Metal leakage was also tested. A certain bulk of metal-CDs was dispersed in 10 mL water and was put into a 500 Da dialysis bag. Another 10 mL water was added outside the dialysis bag. After 24 h storing, the water volume of both inside and outside were tested. And the liquid of both inside and outside were tested for metal content. After detecting, total mass of metal inside and outside were calculated by water volume multiplying concentrations. And the percentage of metal leakage would be calculated.

Standard linear curves were as follow:
Int = 3712.0 × c + 97.8
(
R 2
= 0.999
)
for
copper
content
calculation;
Int = 40596.6 × c + 747.3
(
