= 0.999
)
for
silver
content
calculation.

Int represents intensity which is y axis and c(μg/mL) represents concentration of tested metals which is x axis. The mass fraction (%) were calculated as follow:
m a s s
f r a c t i o n
( % )
=
c × V
1000 × m
× 100 %
(
m g / m g × 100 %
)
V(mL) represents the constant volume and m (mg) represents measured weight for metal-CDs.
Each experiment has at least three replications in one test. Each experiment has a control group which use water or other substance that exclude the bactericide to replace the bactericides.

Fig. A1a shows UV–vis spectra of AgCD and CuCD. Peaks at 270 nm for AgCD and 282 nm for CuCD represent π→π* transition, whose wavelength have a red shift because of the possible long conjugate system of carbon dots. Peaks at 348 nm for AgCD and 410 nm for CuCD represents n→π* transition which are very possible for CO of these carbon dots since their carbon source was glucose. The red shift of n→π* transition may also because the long conjugate system and auxochrome such as hydroxyl. The fluorescent properties were also employed as figure A1b ∼ e shown. Both AgCD and CuCD owns excitation dependence, which means an inhomogenous particle size dispersion. Fig. A1f exhibits quantum yield value calculation of AgCD (1.54%) and CuCD (0.79%), whose values were much lower than common metal-free carbon dots. It may because fluorescence quenching coming from metal. The metal-CDs were prepared as shown in Fig. 1 a, which used inorganic salts including silver nitrate or copper sulfate were used as the metal ion precursors and glucose was applied as the carbon resource. As Fig. 1b shown, XRD image indicates an amorphous structure with crystallinity located at a low degree of both AgCD and CuCD, respectively. Diffraction peak at 24.3° for AgCD) and 24.4° for CuCD) could be assigned to graphite (002) structure themselves [42]. And diffraction peak at 43.3° for AgCD could be assigned to graphitic carbon (101) [43]. As shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. A3a, the particle size of metal-CDs was around 5–20 nm. The particle size of CuCD seemed to be smaller than the size of AgCD with the size of around 5–10 nm. The hydrated particle sizes were both very similar at around 21–23 nm (Table A1), which were in accord with the TEM characterization. In addition, zeta potentials at about −23∼ −24 mV were both obtained as shown in Table A1. And the metal content of the two was also detected by ICP-OES, which showed metal content 0.33% and 0.24% for AgCD and CuCD, respectively. The metal-leakage of metal-CDs were also calculated. After storing 120 days, 8.31% silver drop off from the carbon skeleton and 12.86% copper drop off from the carbon skeleton respectively. Fig. A2 shows the morphology of AgCD and CuCD storing at 0 and 60 days. At day 0, the size of both AgCD and CuCD was around 5–20 nm. And it appears some big particles (nearly 30 nm) at day 60 as TEM images shown. Some particles concentrate more dark ingredients comparing with images at day 0 which may indicate the aggregation of metallic components as the storing time goes by. But the dispersion of particle size was still uniform at day 60 comparing with day 0. Table A2 shows changes of hydrated size and zeta potentials at day 0,30 and 60. As Table A2 shown, hydrated size would increase as storing time goes by. The ratio of small size has been decreased till 60 days. And zeta potential would also change, which means a part of metal may escape from the carbon skeleton combing with the results of TEM images in Fig. A2. As Table A3 shown, MBC would be kept till around 45–60 days for metal-CDs.

The functional groups on the CDs were further studied by FTIR and XPS spectra (Fig. 1d and Fig. A3b). They were nearly identical, indicating a similar chemical reacting progress through microwave synthesis. Peaks at 2920 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibration of alkane. And peaks at 1409 and 1335 cm−1 are ascribed to asymmetrical and symmetrical bending vibration respectively for alkane [32]. Two weak peaks at 1718 and 1617 cm−1 could be assigned to CO stretching vibration for dissociative and associating carbonyl group [44], which assigns to the existence of chromophore of carbonyl combining with the UV–vis analysis results. Usually, the carbonyl one presents a very strong adsorption at around 1700 cm−1, which indicated the carbonyl reacted with other chemicals and had barely residual groups during this reaction. Peak at 3327 cm−1 is O–H bond after association [45], which is in accord with the hydroxyl deduction in UV–vis spectra analysis. Since there are no aldehyde group of characteristic peaks and no nitrogen was contained in precursors, the peak at 1014 cm−1 (1142 and 1069 cm−1 were swallowed up by the main peak) was a mixture of asymmetrical and symmetrical C–O stretching vibration [46]. The weakness of carbonyl and the disappearance of aldehyde indicates the double bonds were broken and the metal atom was complexed with carbon or carbon-oxygen groups. Chemical environment of different atoms was analyzed by XPS spectra. Figs. A3c and A1f show total XPS spectra of AgCD and CuCD that they all have a low metal content and high contents of carbon and oxygen. As Figs. A3d,e,g,h shown, C–O (286.12 eV for AgCD) and (286.22 eV for CuCD) for carbon environment) and a few CO (287.52 eV) existed while CC (283.09 eV for AgCD) and 283.12 eV for CuCD) was appeared [41]. The existence of CC and the red shift in UV–vis spectra of π→π* transition which indicates a long conjugate system in carbon dots including conjugate structure of CC and CC (or CC and CO). Yellow to pale brown colors for these carbon dots water solution can also prove the existence of conjugate structure. The appearance of C–O group at 531.12 eV [47] is in accorded with the FTIR spectra. And CC may be formed with a dehydration process through the reaction. Valence states of metal were also determined by high-resolution XPS spectra. As Fig. 1e shown, 932.20 eV(CuO2) and 951.92 eV could be assigned to the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 electron of Cu+, which indicates Cu–O group with Cu(Ⅰ) [48]. And the lack of satellite peaks of Cu2+ indicated it may be cuprous state [49]. For further confirmation, the modified Auger parameter was calculated which is the sum of the kinetic energy of Cu LMM (Figure A3i, 915.10 eV) and binding energy of Cu 2p3/2 [50]. The value was 1847.20 eV that assigned to the Cu+ rather than two copper species [48,50–52]. Fig. 1f described 366. 58eV (Ag3d3/2) and 372.53 eV (Ag3d1/2) with a difference value of 6 eV [53]. The valence of silver is hard to determined, so Ag MNN Auger peaks were carried out in Fig. A3j. Auger parameter of 3d5/2 M4M45M45 and 3d5/2 M5M45M45 was 723.92 eV and 718.31 eV separately, which assigned with Ag2O for Ag(Ⅰ) [54–57]. Both AgCD and CuCD were detected metal-oxygen bond which is in accord with the decrease of carbonyl peaks in FTIR analysis. And there are obvious C–O bonds in both AgCD and CuCD structure. It suggests that the lone pair electron deriving from CO have been combined with metals, then they formed metal-oxygen bond which belong to carbon dots.

Bactericidal performances of AgCD and CuCD were researched at MBC, taking other bactericides or precursors (for metal-CDs) for comparation study and cell toxicity. 99.9% bactericidal ratio of bactericide was defined as MBC. MBCs of AgCD and CuCD were firstly studied as shown in Fig. 2 a and b which were calculated as around 25 μg/mL (AgCD) and 75 μg/mL (CuCD) respectively. The specific content of metals in metal-CDs were detected by ICP-OES for the purpose of comparing bactericidal effect and cellular toxicity under equivalent metal content. Fig. 2c compared the bactericidal effect between A1/A2 and AgCD. Obviously, the AgCD had an advantage for bactericidal effect rather than A1 and A2 with equivalent Ag content (Fig. 2c). However, the cellular toxicity of AgCD (whose cell viability is higher than 97.9%) could be negligible (Fig. 2e). By contrast, A1(66.3%), A2(77.2%) and silver nitrate (31.8%) all have significant influence for cell viability at equivalent Ag content of AgCD. Similarly, since copper sulfate often employed for sterilizing of fruit tree and swimming pool, the comparison of bactericidal effect between CuCD and copper sulfate was depicted in Fig. 2d. As indicated in XPS characterization, the copper was in the form of Cu+ in the CuCD, which should be the reason for the effective antibacterial effect being comparable with the copper sulfate with equivalent copper content. Nevertheless, in the cellular toxicity study, the cell viability is at least 87.6%, which were higher than copper sulfate at any equivalent concentrations. The cell viability was even down to 20.3% at 200 μg/mL of copper sulfate (Fig. 2e). It can be obtained that the as-obtained metal-CDs has identical or more excellent bactericidal activity while lower cellular toxicity comparing with other common bactericides. It can be supposed that glucose plays as a reductant and a skeletal precursor which could make metal atoms more dispersive. And the oxygen-contained residues may conceal the toxicity to mammal cells at the same time. To further investigate the reasons for bacterial killing, thiourea-modified metal CDs were obtained. As Fig. 2f shown, bactericidal activity of thiourea-treated metal-CDs had extremely shrunk which suggested the metal do play the main role for bacterial killing. Besides, the size and existent state of metal may also influence their bactericidal activity. As Figs. A4 and A5 depicted, both small particle (10–30 nm) and middle particle (60–100 nm) silver or copper nanoparticles have barely bactericidal effect comparing with metal-CDs owning the equivalent metal content. This phenomenon may due to a better dispersibility and smaller size of metal that located at metal-CDs rather than bare metal particles. Moreover, precursors for forming metal-CDs were also investigated by changing different precursors for carbon dots synthesis. As Fig. A6 shown, silver-containing CDs using fructose or galactose has a higher MBC (50–75 μg/mL) comparing with silver-containing CDs using glucose (whose MBC is around 25 μg/mL). And MBCs of copper-containing CDs using fructose or galactose were all beyond 100 μg/mL (Fig. A7). It is indicated that metals are vital for bactericidal effect of metal-CDs. And the specific mechanisms of higher bactericidal effect and lower cellular toxicity would be discussed on the following text. As Figs. A8a and b shown, MBC for sterilizing S. aureus using AgCD and CuCD were both around 50 μg/mL with the final bacterial concentration of 7.5 and 2.5 × 106 CFU/mL, respectively. Results showed in Figure A8c∼f depicted the antibacterial activity of AgCD and CuCD for both E. coli and S. aureus. It has been proved that both AgCD and CuCD have no obvious antibacterial activity.

Morphology and membrane integrity of bacteria were firstly studied for cellular damage performance. SEM and TEM images were presented at Fig. 3 a. The control group has a relative smooth surface and integrated membrane, meanwhile, test groups present wrinkled surface and incomplete membrane. Some bacteria were broken and collapsed and the boundary between different bacterial individuals were blurry. In sliced images, broken cellular membranes and hollow cytoplasm were obviously observed in AgCD and CuCD group, compared with complete membrane and uniform cytoplasm in control group. Fig. 3b shows live/dead kit results which also demonstrate the integrity of bacterial membrane. SYTO9 (green) can enter all state of bacteria regardless of their live or death, while PI (red) can only enter dead ones whose membrane are usually incomplete. To be clear, the green represents alive bacteria and the yellow represents dead ones after the green and red channels merging. The results indicate an effective bactericidal effect for metal-CDs.

Besides, protein leakage and membrane depolarization were also studied for membrane function after sterilizing process. As Fig. 4 a shown, the amount of protein leakage was increased along with the rise of AgCD doses. Similar tendency occurred on CuCD as Fig. 4b depicted. Since the incomplete membrane has been observed by electron microscope directly, membrane depolarization was also studied using a diSC35 dye as another experiment for membrane integrity [58]. The diSC35 dye would be quenched and would stay between inner and outer layers of bacterial membrane when bacteria alive. Once the membrane was depolarized by some bactericides, diSC35 dye would get out of the membrane and recover the fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, fluorescent intensity values were increased by the increase of metal-CDs concentration, but the extremely increase was occurred only at 125 μg/mL which is much higher than MBC of both metal-CDs. In addition, depolarization was proceeded slowly with the extension of sterilizing times. Interestingly, bacterial treated by CuCD has a much lower membrane depolarization level at 0 min comparing to 15 and 30 min, while bacteria treated by AgCD has an extremely high value at 0 min at 125 μg/mL and value differences between different times was not that much comparing to CuCD. It suggested AgCD may has quicker sterilizing effect than CuCD has since AgCD could depolarized bacterial membrane quicker than CuCD did.

To better understand the bactericidal effect on metal-CDs and metal ions, element disperse images and energy disperse spectra (EDS) for treated bacterial cells were detected by a FE-SEM. As Fig. 5 shown, silver (green) or copper (blue) were determined on AgCD or CuCD treated bacteria comparing with the control group (Fig. A9) with a low content of both (Fig. A10 and Fig. A11). Similarly, silver nitrate and copper sulfate treated bacterial cells all present a bit of corresponding metal content just as much as the metal-containing CDs groups obtained (Fig. A12 and Fig. A13). It is indicated that both metal ion and metal-CDs sterilized bacteria with tiny content of metal. Metal elements seems to attach to the cells after treating, but no significant difference has been observed between metal ion and metal-CDs through element disperse images yet.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA damage and energy metabolism disorder
ce:section.ce:para: Except membrane damage, the inner damage was also considered as an important factor for bacterial sterilization. DNA damages can be observed after treated by MBC concentration of metal-CDs staying at different sterilizing duration times. As Fig. 6 a shown, DNA strips have slightly blurred which located at over 5000 bp after treated with AgCD for 15 min and 30 min. However, Fig. 6b showed a significant degradation after treated by CuCD for 15 min and 30 min. Different damage degrees for DNA may also indicated different bactericidal mechanisms between AgCD and CuCD. Lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) as a part of bacterial respiratory chain belongs to bacterial respiratory dehydrogenase which locates at the cytoplasmic side of inner membrane and participates in respiratory metabolism of bacteria through oxidizing organic substrate. Common aerobic respiratory metabolism process includes ATP and solute transport [59]. As Fig. 6c shown, the decrease of LDH viability was along with the increase of concentration of metal-CDs, which proved the inhibition of LDH viability. The viability of LDH for CuCD treated bacteria declined for 50% at MBC concentration, while the viability of LDH for AgCD treated bacteria declined around 25%. It indicates that CuCD may cause more serious inhibition of LDH than AgCD does. Alamar blue assay was employed for detecting cellular metabolic activity for metal-CDs treated bacteria. When cells were damaged and presented low metabolic activity, the relevant activity of coenzyme was also very low, which resulted in a weak signal of fluorescence [40]. As Fig. 6d shown, the signal was declined with the increase of metal-CDs dose. And AgCD group has a more significant decrease than CuCD groups. It indicates that metal-CDs destroy the cellular metabolism for a significant degree.

The increase of total ROS has been proved as a strong index for many bactericides on sterilizing process. Herein, DCFH-DA dye as a probe that could be oxidized by ROS to form dichlorofluorescein. It was observed that metal-CDs-treated bacteria have increase signals with the increase of metal-CDs’ concentration in Fig. 6e. At the concentration of 125 μg/mL, a 4-fold increase and a 2.5-fold increase were presented for AgCD and CuCD respectively according to ones at 0 μg/mL. The accumulation of ROS can destroy membrane integrity through lipid peroxidation. MDA is one of the most distinguished products that derived from cellular lipid membrane peroxidation [60]. Fig. 6f showed an increase trend with the increase of metal-CDs’ concentrations, which means the lipid peroxidation on cellular membrane was enhanced and this phenomenon was induced by increasing metal-CDs’ concentrations. Moreover, GSH as an important oxidative protecting oligopeptide widely distributes in both mammal cells and bacteria. Herein, GSH consumption was also detected by a standard Ellman's assay [58]. 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) dye can react with sulfydryl from GSH and turned into yellow color 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoic acid that could be detected at 412 nm for absorbance. When oxidative stress occurs, GSH would be consumed to protect other enzymes or structures. Fig. A14 showed results for GSH consumption after treated by metal-CDs. GSH has a decrease trend in CuCD treated group but GSH remained detectable value, while AgCD make the GSH changed into a negative value when the concentration larger than 50 μg/mL, which means GSH had already consumed under the concentration over 50 μg/mL. The consumption of GSH caused by AgCD seems faster than CuCD caused. The result is in accord with the membrane depolarization and ROS accumulation, that means AgCD may has a faster speed in sterilizing process than CuCD has. Changes caused by oxidative stress are in accord with the above experiments about cellular damage.

Transcriptome RNA sequencing was used to learn about the antibacterial mechanism at molecular level. Before sequencing, the rRNA ratio (23S/16S) and RNA Integrity Number (RIN) were used to assess the RNA's quality. As shown in Table A4, the percentage of all samples at Q30 were higher than 95% after filtering. And more than 85% clean reads matched the reference genome sequences, indicating that the transcriptome analysis was of good quality. Heat maps show in Figs. A15 and A16 which indicate that the correlation of samples in the same group is high, and the biological duplication groups between different groups can be clearly distinguished. Five groups were designed including the control group, AgCD group, CuCD group, AgNO3 group and CuSO4 group. Meanwhile, GO term enrichment and KEGG pathway enrichment were used to analyze the differences and potential mechanisms in different test groups. DEGs for AgCD group, CuCD group, AgNO3 group and CuSO4 group are obtained comparing with the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptome analysis for silver ions and copper ions treated bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Common bactericidal mechanisms include: destruction of cell walls by contact, damaging protein and nucleic acid by binding to sulfur groups, and induction of ROS by occupying enzyme centers using their intrinsic catalytic performance [10,11,15,16]. Herein, silver nitrate and copper sulfate were employed for figuring out the bactericidal mechanism of silver ion and copper ion. The corresponding GO term enrichment and KEGG pathway enrichment were shown in Figure A17∼A20.

Silver nitrate was compared with the reference(control) groups, 812 DEGs were found, which contains 131 up-regulated DEGs and 681 down-regulated DEGs. As shown in Fig. A17, the first six enrichment terms are all related to cellular biosynthesis, such as macromolecule and organonitrogen compound. And there are five GO terms related to ribosome such as structrual constituent ribosome (GO:0003735), cytosolic ribosome (GO:0022626) and ribonucleoprotein complex (GO:1990904). KEGG enrichment also shows ribosome relative terms at the top (ko03010) of Fig. A18, whose term contains 30 DEGs with the Q-value of 9.7 × 10−5. The enriched expression levels of DEGs all are down-regulated, indicating a loss of ribosome function that further prevents the translation progress, which is consistent with other reports that silver ion can combine with 30s subunit of ribosome [10,11,33,61]. DEGs enriched at photosynthesis term (ko00195) all are related to energy metabolism, and the expression of these DEGs are all down-regulated (Table A5). Amino acid synthesis and metabolism (ko00290, ko00400) are significantly influenced, and the biosynthesis of valine, leucine, phenylalanine, tyosine, histidine and other amino acid is seriously inhibited because the expressions of relative DEGs all are down-regulated (Table A5). Besides, peptidoglycan biosynthesis (ko00550) pathway and O-antigen nucleotide sugar biosynthesis (ko00541) pathway were inhibited (Table A5). The downregulation of peptidoglycan, O-antigen nucleotide sugar and related DEGs may reveal the disability in cell wall formation. In addition, biosynthesis of nucleotide sugar (ko00520 and ko01250) was inhibited (Table A5), which means that the synthesis of deoxyribonucleic acid or ribonucleic acid failed. Obviously, silver nitrate destroyed the function of ribosome and caused the synthesis and degradation of amino acid. And further prevent the synthesis of cell wall and nucleotide leading to bacterial death.

Copper sulfate group was compared with the control group. 500 DEGs were found, which contains 159 up-regulated DEGs and 341 down-regulated DEGs. In GO term enrichment (Fig. A19), ribosome and biosynthetic processes were the two main enrichment terms. Structural constituent of ribosome (GO:0003735), cytosolic ribosome (GO:0022626), ribosomal subunit (GO: 0044391) and rRNA binding (GO:0019843) were all associated with ribosome terms which contains 21 down-regulated DEGs (Table A6). The terms associated with biosynthesis in bacterial cells are structural molecule activity (GO:0005198), cellular biosynthetic process (GO:0044249) and biosynthetic process (GO:0009058). These three terms contain 150 DEGs, of which 118 have down-regulated gene expressional levels. Preliminarily, the bactericidal mechanism of copper sulfate was to inhibit ribosome function and reduce biosynthesis. Further, KEGG enrichment gives more information shown in Fig. A20. Ribosome (ko03010), biosynthesis of amino acids (ko01230) and ABC transporter (ko02010) are the top three enrichment terms. DEGs enriched in KEGG ribosome pathway were nearly identical with the ribosome pathway in GO term (Table A6). The biosynthesis of Tryptophan (trpA/B/D), threonine, serine(thrA/B), leucine(leuA/B/C/D), histidine (hisA/C/D/F/I) and other amino acids were inhibited. There were 31 DEGs in biosynthesis of amino acids pathway (ko01230), 29 of which were down-regulated (Table A4). The pathway of ABC transporter contains 41 DEGs and 40 of which were down-regulated (Table A6). The transportation of small-molecule sugar (ytfT/R/Q, mglA/B/C, malE/F/J), peptide (sapC/F, dppA/B/C/D/F) and phosphate (pst A/B/C/S) were prevented. Consequently, the inhibition of ribosome function given rise to reduced biosynthesis of amino acid, which, coupled with the restraint of sugar, peptide and phosphate transporter, ultimately caused bacterial cells death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptome analysis for metal-CDs treated bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Transcriptome analysis was performed for both AgCD and CuCD treated bacteria separately. DEGs in AgCD group or CuCD group were firstly filtered, then GO term and KEGG pathway enriching analysis for both groups were carried out respectively. In the case of AgCD group, totally 908 DEGs were found, which contains 505 up-regulated DEGs and 403 down-regulated DEGs as Fig. 7 a shown. The volcano plots were carried out in Fig. 7b to describe the distribution of DEGs. Under the same distinguishing standard, totally 791 DEGs were found for CuCD-treated bacteria, which contains 385 up-regulated DEGs and 406 down-regulated DEGs as Fig. 7e shown. The volcano plots were carried out at Fig. 7f to describe the distribution of DEGs that were treated by hierarchical clustering analysis. GO term enrichment and KEGG pathway enrichment were used to analyzed DEGs for potential bactericidal mechanisms for AgCD. GO term was classified for three functional categories, which are molecular function (MF), cellular component (CC) and biological progress (BP). In both cases of AgCD and CuCD, DEGs belonging to GO classification for three different categories have much intersection which is meaningless for typical mechanism analysis separately. For example, AgCD group has 705 DEGs in BP, 639 DEGs in CC and 726 DGEs in MF among a total 907 DEGs set. As a result, GO term enrichment and KEGG pathway enrichment was focused on.

In AgCD group, the most enriching GO terms (Fig. 7c) includes regulation of transcription, DNA-templated (GO: 0006355), regulation of biosynthetic process (GO: 0009889), response to inorganic substance (GO: 0010035), response to metal ion (GO: 0010038) regulation of macromolecule biosynthetic process (GO: 0010556) and regulation of nucleobase-containing compound metabolic process (GO: 0019219). KEGG terms were distributed at five pathway terms, which are cellular processes, environmental information processing, genetic information processing, metabolism and organismal systems. KEGG classification shows that most of DEGs focus on metabolism terms (Fig. 7d). KEGG enrichment analysis suggested several main terms which includes histidine metabolism (ko00340), biosynthesis of amino acids (ko01230), fatty acid degradation (ko00071), valine, leucine and isoleucine degradation/biosynthesis (ko00280 and ko00290), sulfur metabolism (ko00920), ABC transporter (ko02010) and RNA polymerase(ko03020).

In CuCD group for GO term enrichment (Fig. 7f), metabolism and biosynthetic process of nucleoside monophosphate (GO: 0009156, 0009161, 0009123, 0009124), metabolism and biosynthetic process of purine nucleoside/ribonucleside monophosphate (GO: 0009126, 0009127, 0009167, 0009168) and inosinemonphosphate (IMP, GO: 0006188, 0006189, 0046040) were most enriched. DEGs in these GO terms were up-regulated for most of them which may indicate nucleotide metabolism disorder. For KEGG pathway enrichment (Fig. 7h), arginine and folate biosynthesis (ko00220, ko00790), one carbon pull by folate (ko00670), alanine, aspartate and glutamate metabolism (ko00250) and purine and pyrimidine metabolism (ko00230, 00240) are most enriched. The upregulation of metabolism of purine and pyrimidine and folate formation and utilization may suggest degradation of nucleic acid and insufficient source for nucleic acid biosynthesis. Different from AgCD group, most DEGs belonging to the top of these GO term and KEGG pathway terms were up-regulated and were focused on metabolism or biosynthesis of nucleoside monophosphate which may be segmentary for bactericidal analysis. In order to understanding the contribution of downregulated DEGs, up-regulated and down-regulated DEGs were enriched alone by GO term and KEGG pathway enrichment respectively (Tables A7 and A8). For CuCD group (Table A7) with down-regulated DEGs, membrane (GO:0016020) and membrane relevant terms such as integral component of membrane (GO:0016021) and intrinsic component of membrane (GO:0031224) were most enriched. And KEGG enrichment for the total 158 DEGs about membrane, several pathways were obtained including phosphotransferase system (ko02060, PTS), ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter (ko02010), and two-component system (ko02020, TCS).

The most remarkable DEGs enriched in metabolic functional relative pathways occurring on the cases of both AgCD group and CuCD group. As shown in Fig. 9a and e, typical metabolism relative DEGs were listed.

In AgCD groups, the biosynthesis of various amino acid was inhibited. In terms of histidine metabolism (ko00340), all seven DEGs were downregulated significantly. In biosynthesis of amino acids terms, ilvM (acetolactate synthase II small subunit) had nearly zero expression while ilvH (acetolactate synthase I/III small subunit) was down-regulated for 4-fold. The loss of ilvH expression is involved in several amino metabolic processes, which may lead to declined amino acids synthesis. Tryptophan biosynthesis gene like trp A\B\C\D\E (GO:0000162) were down-regulated for 15-fold and 4.15-fold, and the biosynthesis of phenylalanine, tyrosine and tryptophan (ko00400) were also influenced. The synthesis and metabolism of leucine (leuC and leuD) and histidine (hisA and hisF) were inhibited, too. In addition, the biosynthesis of threonine (GO:0009088) and the metabolism of threonine, glycine and serine (thrA, thrB and thrC) were also inhibited(ko00260). Fatty acid degradation (ko00071) was also affected, with down-regulation of fadA, fadB, fadI and fadJ. The pathway map (Fig. A21) indicated that this would reduce the amount of acetyl-coenzymeA (CoA), further blocking the tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA) and decreasing energy metabolism. Down-regulation of fadA and fadI also inhibits degradation of ethylbenzene (ko00642), thereby reducing the production of acetyl-CoA and thus affect ingpyruvate metabolism (Fig. 8 a).

In CuCD groups, nucleotide, nucleic acid and their relative amino acids are the main substances responsible for metabolism disorder as depicted in Fig. 9e. In GO term enrichment analysis, the genes related to nucleoside monophosphate biosynthesis and metabolism were almost identical and up-regulated. In KEGG pathway enrichment, metabolism of alanine, aspartate and glutamate (pyrB, putA, purF, purB, glnA, gdhA, ansA and ansB) were up-regulated. And aspartate and glutamate are necessary components for purine biosynthesis [62]. Glycine, serine and threonine metabolism (serB, serA, sdaA, sdaB, glyA, gcvT, gcvP and gcvH) were up-regulated which are important amino acid for forming one carbon unit [63]. In E. coli cells, a carbon unit participates in biosynthesis of purines and pyrimidines, requiring tetrahydrofolic acid as a carrier. And tetrahydrofolic acid could be synthesized from folate acid. Further, one carbon pool by folate (purN, purH, folD and folA) and folate biosynthesis (pabB, pabA, folE, folB and folA) were activated (Fig. 8b) [64,65].Besides, the expression of 20 DEGs in purine metabolism pathway (ko00230) and 14 DEGs in pyrimidine pathways (ko00240) were also up-regulated. DEGs such as purT/M/K/L/D/B, hpt, guaA and adk, which were enriched in purine metabolism pathway, regulate the synthesis of synthetase or ligase And DEGs like carA, carB, pyrB, pyrD and udk regulate the synthesis of synthetase and transferase for pyrimidine biosynthesis.

According GO term enrichment (Fig. 7c), relevant DEGs for nucleoside monophosphate biosynthesis was up-regulated resulting in more nucleotide production. However, there needs nucleoside triphosphate to change into DNA or RNA by polymerase catalyzing [66–68]. DEGs of regulated DNA or RNA polymerase were not significantly up-regulated. On the contrary, expressions of holA, umuC and umuD were down-regulated for 2.99-fold, 6.36-fold and 3.20-fold respectively which relate to the formation of DNA polymerase. And the expression of holD (which is a DNA polymerase regulated DEG) was up-regulated for 2.97-fold. The opposite regulating direction means incomplete polymerase. Moreover, RNA degradation was promoted by rhlE, pfkA, pcnB and deaD, whileRNA biosynthesis (GO:2001141) was found decreased by 47 downregulated DEGs. Among these 47 DEGs, the expression of kdpE, sdiA, flgM, atoC, atoS and dcuS were down-regulated which indicate the inhibition of signal transduction(ko02020). And DEGs of pantothenate and CoA biosynthesis (mazG, perA and perC) were also down-regulated for 3.24-fold, 2.88-fold and 3.91-fold respectively. Downregulation of DEGs about DNA polymerase, pantothenate and CoA along with the upregulation of DEGs about nucleoside monophosphate biosynthesis such as folate biosynthesis are contradictory results for nucleoside biosynthesis. It means the ribonucleic acid or deoxyribonucleic acid synthesis needs may not be satisfied in nucleoside triphosphate level though the efforts were struggled for nucleoside monophosphate synthesis.

In addition to metabolism problems, membrane transport is also a vital aspect of bactericidal effectiveness. As shown in Fig. 9b and f, with the abnormal transport of sugar, peptide, metal ions and loss of some functions of the enzyme, the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter and two-component system are most affected (Table A7)

AgCD group was firstly analyzed. ABC transporters are essential for bacteria survival and play an important role in nutrient substance, secreting toxins and excreting antibiotics or other harmful substances [69–71]. AgCD group enriched with 51 DEGs, which were mainly involved in transmembrane transport (Fiurge 9b). Among them, 39 down-regulated DEGs related to sugar transportation such as ytfT/R/Q, yphF/E, xylG/F, mglA/B, malK/F/E/B, sulfur transportation (cysW/U/P/C), peptide transportation (dppF/D/C/B/A and sapB/C/D/F) and lysine/arginine/ornithine transportation (argT). The inhibiting tendency effected on transmembrane transport is in accord with the decline of energy metabolism that may cause the inhibition of transportations. In CuCD group, transportation of galatofuranose (ytfR/T/A), cationic peptide(sapF/C), ribose (rbsC/A), glutathione(gsiD) and microcin C (yejF/E/B) were all inhibited. DEGs encode two enzymes, ATP-binding protein and permease, showing a reduction in the transport of not only nutrients but also toxins.

Another family of membrane transporters is the two-component system (TCS), which helps bacteria adapt quickly to environmental conditions such as osmotic pressure, different temperatures, environmental viscosity and carbon sources. TCS usually consist of a sensor histidine kinase and a response regulator. A stimulus signal received by histidine kinase firstly, and then ATP-dependent histidine residues are phosphorylated to activate the domain of regulator for further step [72–74]. As Fig. 9b shown, 19 DEGs were enriched including flagellar motility DEGs (flgM, flhC, flhD and fliA), fatty acid metabolism DEGs (atoS and atoC), metal efflux DEGs (cusC and cusF). Short chain fatty acid metabolism was inhibited significantly since the expressions of atoS and atoC were down-regulated for 12.9-fold and 8.5-fold, which were consistent with the down-regulation of fatty acid degredation (ko00071). More importantly, copper and silver transport relative DEGs (comR, copA, cueO, cusC and cusF) which belongs to TCS, were up-regulated significantly. Copper/silver export system periplasmic binding protein was regulated by cusF, and copper/silver out membrane channel was regulated by cusC. Combined with the up-regulation of cueO (p-type Cu+ transporter) and copA (blue copper oxidase), the efflux of silver or copper increases [60,61,75]. Besides, zinc efflux (znuB and znuC) is also stimulated as previously mentioned. Although silver is not an inherent ion of E. coli, the invasion of silver can also induce ion efflux. Combined with metabolism and membrane transport DEGs, total metabolism inhibition and ion pump failure were the main causes of bacterial death in AgCD treated groups.

In CuCD groups (Fig. 9f), potassium transportation wasinhibited since the function of sensor histidine (kdpD, −4.92-fold), ATPase subunit (kdpC, −9.2-fold), the synthesis of ATP-binding subunit (kdpB, −17.1-fold) and potassium binding subunit (kdpA, −32-fold) were prevented. Other expressions of DEGs related to histidine kinase or transporters (atoS, dcuB and dcuS) were also down-regulated. Similarly, cusR/cusS system relative DEGs were also up-regulated in CuCD group which has the same trend in AgCD groups. This system is a two-component regulatory system for copper reaction. Copper ion was detected by cusS and a phosphate group was given for cusR in order to activate cusCFBA and cusRS operon. copA and cueO were also help cells efflux copper [60]. Metal-CDs can stimulate ionic responses in both metallic and nonmetallic efflux. Silver or copper is mostly excreted as a reaction to metal stimulant.

In membrane transport, there is another important term named phosphotransferase system (PTS) in CuCD groups. PTS is a member of translocator family and is participated in multiple sugar uptake and phosphorylation. It consists of a heat-stable protein (HPr) containing histidine and two enzymes (EⅠ and EⅡ). Only when HPr was supported by EⅡ, HPr, could transfer sugars and control the carbohydrate metabolism [PTS-10,11,12]. The transduction of some sugars such as fructose, mannose (cmtB, srlA, srlB and srlE), galactitol(sgcC) and cellobiose(chbB) transduction were inhibited since expressions of relative DEGs were down-regulated. And all these DEGs regulated EⅡ. It indicates a decrease of sugar transduction. In addition, peptidoglycan biosynthesis (pbpC and mtgA) and biofilm formation (pgaA/B/C and dgaE) were also declined that regulated by several DEGs, which may damage the function of bacterial membrane. Inhibition of ABC transporter and TCS both suggested decreased bacterial adaptive capacity and impaired membrane function.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular mobility and protection of oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular mobility and oxidative stress were also enriched at the top 20 of GO and KEGG enrichment. In AgCD group, biofilm formation-Escherichia coli (ko02026), longevity regulating pathway (ko04213) and peroxisome (ko04146) were the most enriched KEGG terms after enriching for the top ten GO enrichment terms. In AgCD group, flagellum related DEGs (flgM, flhC, flhD and fliA) were enriched both in biofilm formation term and flagellar assembly term(ko02040) as Fig. 9b depicted, indicated that flagellar activity is essential for bacterial cell mobility. Negative regulator of flagellin synthesis was encoded by flgM whose expression was down-regulated for 9.64-fold. Flagellar transcriptional activator was encoded by flhC and flhD whose expressions were also down-regulated for 3.1-fold and 4.4-fold respectively [75]. Meanwhile, the expression of fliA (flagellar RNA polymerase sigma factor) was up-regulated for 4.75-fold. Though bacteria were stimulated to upregulate the synthesis of flagellum (flgM, fliA), it was failed at transcriptional stage (flhC and flhD). Oxidative stress relative DEGs may influence the cell lifetime and biofilm formation. As shown in Fig. 9c, oxyR participated in peroxide metabolism and protection whose expression was up-regulated for 3.8-fold. Other DEGs like sodB (up-regulated 4.3-fold) and sodC (down-regulated 3.6-fold) also regulates redox reactions. They can convert O2- to H2O2 and then to O2, detoxifying reactive oxygen species [61]. Even so, oxidative stress was detected using a ROS kit at the beginning of sterilization.

In KEGG enrichment for CuCD group, flagellar assembly pathway (ko02040) was enriched significantly. Flagellar biosynthesis (flgN and flhB) was inhibited. As shown in Fig. 9g, expressions of flgN and flhB were down-regulated for 10.3-fold and 3.6-fold respectively. Flagellar metor stator element DEGs (motA and motB) were also down-regulated. The expression of another biosynthesis related DEGs (fliZ)was down-regulated for 5.2-fold. These down-regulated DEGs stock down the motility of the flagella. In addition, negative regulator of flagellin synthesis and peptidoglycan hydrolase were down-regulated which means a protecting intention for flagellum damage. Besides, several relevant DEGs associated with oxidative stress. The recX gene (down-regulated 3.7-fold) is an inhibitor of recA, which is responsible for DNA recombination and indicates a protective effect on DNA [61,76]. DEGs for oxidative stress protection were up-regulated such as katG, ahpC, ahpF and aroA which involved in peroxide metabolism and oxidation resistance. On the other hand, superoxide dismutase (sodC) and glutathione transport system permease (gsiD) were inactivated, indicating the damage of oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unique mechanisms for AgCD and CuCD
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the above three aspects, AgCD and CuCD have their own unique mechanisms. For AgCD's unique DEGs (Fig. A22), the top KEGG enrichment term was PTS (ko02060) compared to silver nitrate in Fig. A23. Other most enrichment terms were pentose phosphate pathway(ko00030) and galactose metabolism (ko00052), which means there may be a unique bactericidal mechanism that related to sugar metabolism for AgCD. PTS is responsible for the phosphorylation and uptake of various sugars and controls of carbohydrate metabolism. PTS formed by a histidine-containing protein (HPr) and two different enzymes called enzymeⅠ and enzymeⅡ. HPr is involved in the distribution of high-energy phosphates from enzymeⅠ to different sugar transporters. And the enzymeⅡ that forms several sugar transporter operon such as fruBKA operaon, chbBCARFC operon and srlAEBD operon are involved in sugar translocation [77]. As shown in Fig. 9d, there were 13 unique DEGs enriched in PTS, almost all of which were related to the regulation of enzymeⅡ, and nine of them were up-regulated. It indicates that an increase of sugar translocation, and the decrease of histidine metabolism (Fig. 9a) may be a protection for HPr. However, the decrease of amino acid biosynthesis may influence the integrity of HPr. Though several sugar such as galactose (gatA, agaC and agaD), fructose (fruB, fruK, furA),cellose (chbC) for sugar translocation regulating by enzymeⅡ were up-regulated by required DEGs, expressions of many sugar transportation relative DEGs were down-regulated (ytfT/R/Q, yphF/E, xylG/F, mglA/B, malK/F/E/B) in ABC transporter terms, which suggest the source depletion of sugars. In another words, although PTS are willing to translocate sugar to provide phosphorylated sugars, there is barely no sugar available for PTS to use due to the inhibitory effect of sugars on ABC transporters. Moreover, pentose phosphate pathway and galactose metabolism were activated may also indicate insufficient carbon source. Interestingly, although many enrichment terms and genes regulation pointed to problems with sugar metabolism, no relevant glucose metabolism or transport DEGs were screened. We supposed that glucose structural analogues derived from glucose precursors may deceive bacteria in recognizing our AgCDs as a macromolecular sugar. Actually, the synthesized AgCDs retained some glucose residues, such as hydroxyl and carbonyl (proved by FT-IR and XPS analysis). The reservation of organic groups may be due to the very short synthesis time using microwave oven.

In CuCD groups, metabolism disorder of nucleotide and nucleic acid has been most enriched of GO term enrichment. In addition, the inhibition of transcription may also be an important mechanism of bacterial death. In GO term enrichment of down-regulated DEGs (Table A7), negative regulation of protein-containing complex disassembly (GO: 0060567), transcription activity (GO:0140110), regulation of transcription (GO:0006355) and regulation of RNA biosynthetic process (GO:2001141) were significant enriched. DEGs in these different GO terms are overlapped. Cold shock protein can help cells overcome stressful environments and inhibit misfolding of mRNA secondary structures (cspA and cspB) at low temperatures [78–80]. Moreover, cold shock proteins also work under normal conditions and are participated in DNA recombination and superspin, ribosome assembly and mRNA binding [79,80]. As shown in Fig. 9h–six cold shock proteins regulating DEGs (cspA/B/C/E/G/I) were down-regulated in CuCD groups, indicating that t they lost their protective effect against physiological stress and normal function of nucleic acid synthesis. In addition, there are also many DEGs regulating various transcriptional regulation factors that related to carbohydrate utilization (hexR and yebK), oligosaccharide metabolism (such as rhamnose, disaccharide, glucose and lactose; DEGs: rhaR, melR, srlR, bglJ, lacI, galS, rbsR and sfsA) and amino acid metabolism (lgoR, sgcR, gadX, leuO). Down-regulation of these DEGs is consistent with the inhibition of carbohydrate transport in membrane transport analysis (Fig. 9f), adding to the difficulty of positive transcription regulation. Besides, the sulfur relay system term (ko04122) revealed down-regulation of 2-thiouridine proteins (tusB/C/D) which influence the synthesis of t-RNA. Transcription activity was significantly inhibited, which may be indirect evidence of DNA degradation as shown by DNA gel electrophoresis (Fig. 6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion of bactericidal mechanisms for metal-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: AgCD influences the membrane transport and inhibits low-molecular carbohydrate and peptide. With the decrease of sugar transportation, phosphotransferase system was activated in order to utilize various sugar for energy production. However, the metabolism of amino acids, fatty acids and sugars, especially fatty acids, has been already affected. The suppression of fatty acid metabolism causes the decline of acetyl coenzyme A, which significantly strikes down tricarboxylic acid cycle [27,30], and can further destroy aerobic respiration and energy metabolism [17]. Frustration of flagellar synthesis and activation of oxidative stress protective genes were also indicated the deduction mentioned above. Besides, membrane transportation improved the efflux of ion metal because of the stimulation of silver [17]. According to the expression of DEGs in various enrichment terms, the main bactericidal mechanism is that the abnormal membrane transport leads to the disturbance of amino acid and sugar metabolism, which further causes insufficient energy metabolism and bacterial cell death [27,31].

For CuCD group, the main reason for bacterial death is the disorder of amino acid metabolism, the interference of transport and the obstruction of membrane transport function. In amino acid metabolism, the synthesis of folic acid, one carbon unit and nucleoside monophosphate are up-regulated. The synthesis of nucleotide chains is inhibited while the degradation of nucleotide chains is induced at the same time, which shows the need of synthesis of chain nucleotides (DNA/RNA) is not satisfied. In addition, disruption of nucleotide metabolism that inhibits RNA synthesis is accompanied by the obstruction of transcription process [27]. As for membrane function, the transportation of sugars, amino acids, oligopeptide and other small molecules was decreased. The expression of DEGs related to integrity of membrane function and the expression of bacterial motility relative DEGs for flagellar motility were down-regulated. It indicates that the transport of essential substances for bacterial cellular biosynthesis was not only hindered, but also the adaptability to harsh environments was weaken. Finally, some up-regulated DEGs were enriched, including the DEGs that should stimulate the protective effect of oxidation, and the DEGs related to copper ion efflux [17,25,27]. Changes on these DEGs suggest that the bacteria are responding to an environment that threatens their survival, but this may be an observed phenomenon rather than the primary mechanism of sterilization.

Scheme 1 shows the total possible bactericidal mechanisms of metal-CDs. Both AgCD group and CuCD group present metabolic disorders in the transport of amino acids, fatty acids or nucleotides. This analysis accorded with the results of Alamar blue assay to determine the decrease of cell viability. Moreover, the CuCD group has a unique nucleotide metabolism disorder in further inhibiting transcriptional regulation, which may be the reason why CuCD group has more serious damage to DNA degradation than AgCD group. Membrane transport will be the first to be influenced. The transportation of sugar, peptide, amino acid and ions were inhibited because of the DEGs related to PTS, TCS and ABC transporter pathways were down-regulated. The down-regulated expression of these DEGs affect both function and integrity of membrane. And both metal-CDs treated bacteria presented different degree of depolarization of the membrane and incomplete membrane by experiments such as membrane depolarization assay, protein leakage, live/dead fluorescent staining and electron-microscopy observation. In addition, LDH assay results may be consistent with the inhibition of ABC transporter pathway, which may lead to a decline in aerobic respiration and ATP transport. Synthesis and function of flagella were also hindered according to down-regulated relative DEGs, which keep bacteria away from bactericide. Moreover, the expression of metal ion efflux relative DEGs were up-regulated for the stimulating of metal- CDs, but could not prevent the cellular death. And the increase of total ROS and MDA were detected in metal-CDs treated bacteria which means oxidative stress occurred when sterilization. The expression of relative DEGs for oxidative stress protection were up-regulated. And total GSH was consumed by the stimulating of metal-CDs. In a word, metal-CDs hurt membrane transport firstly and further influenced the metabolism of amino acid, sugar, peptide or nucleotide. The disorder of membrane transport and metabolic inhibition were the possible reasons of bacteria death. Oxidative stress and metal ion efflux were only phenomenon but not basic causes. Unlike the direct prohibition of ribosome function by silver or copper ions, the bactericidal mechanisms of metal-CDs were more complicated and the bactericidal route was various without obvious ribosome toxicity, but similar bactericidal behaviors could also be observed. In cellular toxicity experiment, metal-CDs show tiny toxicity while has the equal or better bactericidal effect. Both bacterial and mammalian cells own ribosome, but mammalian cells have more abundant pathway for life-sustaining activity than bacterial cells, which corresponds to the less toxicity of metal-CDs.

Two kinds of metal-containing CDs were synthesized for bacterial killing successfully. In the same metal content, compared with domestic metal-containing bactericides, its bactericidal activity is the best, and compared with other commonly used bactericides, its cytotoxicity is lower. In order to clarify the specific mechanism of these two metal-CDs, various bactericidal factors and mRNA transcriptome were carried out. Transcriptomic analysis elucidates the different inhibitory mechanisms of metal ions (silver or copper ions) and metal-containing CDs. The similarities and differences of AgCD and CuCD in bactericidal mechanisms were compared. Silver and copper ions directly attack ribosomes causing bacterial death, while AgCD and CuCD inhibits the metabolism of amino acid, fatty acids or nucleotides, and obstruct membrane transport instead of influencing ribosome directly. Besides, CuCD inhibits transcription, and AgCD can trick bacteria into sugar transport using glucose residue CDs. Generally speaking, the smaller size, better dispersion to the metal, and the presence of oxygen residues should achieve the purpose of greater effect and less toxicity [10,30,33]. Mammalian cells are less cytotoxic and more bactericidal, probably because the metal-CDs does not directly interfere with the ribosomes that both bacteria and mammalian cells own, but affects cell metabolism in a number of different ways which mammalian cells have more abundant metabolic pathways than bacteria. As a result, compared with the corresponding metal ions, the metal containing on the carbon dots does change its bactericidal effect, enhance its bactericidal effect and reduce the cytotoxicity of the metal.
